chunk_summary: This section discusses the indications, thresholds, and methods for fortifying human milk, particularly for preterm infants, to meet their nutritional needs, including specific caloric and protein intake targets.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional inadequacies of unfortified human milk for very preterm infants.
Learn the criteria and thresholds for initiating milk fortification in preterm infants.
Familiarize with the preparation and safety protocols for fortified milk.
Recognize alternative fortification practices in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Unfortified human milk does not meet the protein (3.5–4.5 g/kg/day) and caloric (110–130 kcal/kg/day) needs of very preterm infants.
Routine fortification is recommended for infants <34 weeks’ gestation or <1500 g once they tolerate 100 mL/kg/day of enteral feeds.
Fortification typically uses multi-nutrient human milk fortifiers to increase energy, protein, calcium, and phosphorus without increasing fluid volume.
Powdered fortifiers are not sterile; preparation must follow clean techniques, and fortified milk should be used within defined time limits.
In India, preterm formula powder is sometimes used as a substitute for commercial fortifiers under dietitian oversight due to cost and availability issues.
reference_quote: Routine fortification is therefore indicated for most infants <34 weeks’ gestation or <1500 g once they tolerate moderate enteral volumes.
document_key_topics: Safety and preparation of fortified milk
